meta.title: University Copilot Institution Transformation Simulator
meta.description: This JSON file defines a staged Goguen-style institutional model for the implementation of Copilot across a university. It preserves the shell-compatible schema while reinterpreting the controls, presets, stages and Monte Carlo scoring logic around signatures, sentences, models and satisfaction relations in the transformation from imposed ambient AI to a more coherent, demand-led educational institution.

controls.label: Ambient Copilot intrusion across platforms
controls.help: High values mean Copilot appears throughout email, documents, note-taking and other everyday tools, shaping the feel of the university's digital environment.

controls.label: Difficulty of opting out
controls.help: High values mean staff and students cannot easily disable or avoid Copilot in ordinary workflows.

controls.label: Policy ambiguity on legitimate AI use
controls.help: High values mean uncertainty about what counts as acceptable use in teaching, feedback, assessment and student work.

controls.label: Assessment fragility under ambient AI
controls.help: High values mean many assessments still assume older authorship conditions and are easily destabilised by generative AI.

controls.label: Vendor lock-in and ecosystem dependence
controls.help: High values mean the institution is tightly coupled to the Microsoft platform and struggles to configure alternatives or differentiated pathways.

controls.label: Visibility of actual educational demand
controls.help: Higher values mean the university distinguishes learning demand, support demand, assessment demand and failure demand rather than treating everything as AI adoption.

controls.label: Local academic discretion and agency
controls.help: Higher values mean departments, programmes and teaching teams can shape rules and practices in line with disciplinary purpose.

controls.label: Student agency and perceived choice
controls.help: Higher values mean students feel able to choose how they learn and are not simply pushed into ambient AI use.

controls.label: Capability to support learning without over-reliance on AI
controls.help: Higher values mean staff, study support and digital education teams can meet real educational needs in coherent ways.

controls.label: Depth of consultation and co-design
controls.help: Higher values mean staff and students help define the problem and shape decisions rather than being informed after the fact.

controls.label: Competitive and efficiency pressure from leadership
controls.help: High values mean leaders prioritise speed, visibility, cost optics and future-readiness narratives over studying educational work.

controls.label: Political resistance and backlash intensity
controls.help: High values mean unions, senate, departments, students or middle managers are contesting the change.

controls.label: Governance and compliance anxiety
controls.help: High values mean academic standards, data protection and reputational concerns are interpreted defensively, often adding control layers.

controls.label: Strength of mitigation and trust-repair mechanisms
controls.help: Higher values mean pilots, transparent evidence, opt-out routes, apology, layered governance and shared review are active.

controls.label: Redesign effort and leadership commitment
controls.help: Higher values mean the institution is redesigning assessment, guidance, configuration and governance rather than just messaging the rollout better.

stages.name: Imposed ambient AI
stages.description: Copilot is embedded as a fait accompli; agency is low and hidden contradiction accumulates.

stages.name: Backlash and failure demand
stages.description: Queries, disputes, workaround labour and anger become visible across teaching, support and governance.

stages.name: Diagnostic consultation
stages.description: The institution studies demand, distinguishes failure demand from value demand, and reframes the problem.

stages.name: Negotiated redesign
stages.description: Pilots, local variation and layered governance coexist with active political contest over the future shape of AI use.

stages.name: Coherent demand-led university
stages.description: AI is optional where needed, used where valuable, and governed around educational purpose, standards and trust.

riskRules.title: Loss-of-agency backlash
riskRules.body: High ambient presence with little opt-out is likely to intensify the sense that teaching and learning have been reorganised without consent.

riskRules.title: Assessment legitimacy crisis
riskRules.body: Weak assessment design under high policy ambiguity is likely to produce disputes over authorship, misconduct and the meaning of academic standards.

riskRules.title: Managerial rollback or hardening
riskRules.body: Political contest is likely to trigger more central control, stronger messaging and defensive governance unless mitigation remains visible.

riskRules.title: Defensive compliance loop
riskRules.body: Governance anxiety may be translated into extra controls, declarations and approvals, increasing failure demand rather than clarifying purpose.

riskRules.title: Vendor capture perception
riskRules.body: High platform dependence makes the change look less like educational redesign and more like the institutionalisation of a vendor ecosystem.

riskRules.title: Surface consultation without real change
riskRules.body: A review may gather views without altering the decision space if consultation is shallow and redesign effort remains weak.

riskRules.title: Kick-back is currently more containable
riskRules.body: Conflict still exists, but layered governance, opt-out routes and visible co-design make sustained transformation more plausible.

visibility.title: Study educational demand at the point of use
visibility.body: Map where students and staff actually get stuck. Separate value demand such as explanation, feedback and fair assessment from failure demand caused by ambiguity, rework and governance confusion.

redesign.title: Redesign guidance, assessment and configuration together
redesign.body: Do not treat policy as separate from pedagogy or platform settings. Reduce ambient intrusion where it is not needed, redesign fragile assessments, and give departments concrete room to adapt practice.

politics.title: Stabilise the politics of the transformation
politics.body: Use apology, transparent evidence, bounded pilots, explicit no-pilot zones and shared governance to reduce the sense that AI is simply being imposed from above.

clientPath.title: Rebuild student and staff agency
clientPath.body: Create meaningful choice, clearer declarations, concrete examples of acceptable use, and support routes that do not assume Copilot must mediate every educational interaction.

S1.title: Treat complaints as system knowledge
S1.body: Do not explain backlash as mere resistance to change. Use repeat queries, senate objections, staff workarounds and student anger to diagnose structural failure demand.

S2.title: Shift from study to bounded redesign
S2.body: Move from consultation into visible changes: opt-out where feasible, local rules with central non-negotiables, and pilots tied to educational purpose rather than vendor capability.

S3.title: Protect the redesign from recentralisation
S3.body: This stage is politically delicate. Keep evidence public, preserve local discretion, and resist turning successful pilots into a renewed universal rollout story.

S4.title: Treat coherence as dynamic, not final
S4.body: Keep monitoring new forms of failure demand, subtle drift in assessment practice, and renewed pressure for institution-wide platform expansion.

mechanismTexts.internal: function(v, summary){ return `Ambient intrusion (${v.ambientIntrusion}), opt-out difficulty (${v.optOutDifficulty}) and policy ambiguity (${v.policyAmbiguity}) are ${summary.failure.mean > 55 ? 'driving a large volume of avoidable educational and administrative demand' : 'less dominant than in the imposed-rollout state'}. Raising demand visibility (${v.demandVisibility}), academic agency (${v.localAcademicAgency}) and redesign effort (${v.redesignEffort}) weakens this loop.`; }
mechanismTexts.client: function(v, summary){ return `Student agency (${v.studentAgency}) and support capability (${v.supportCapability}) shape whether people can respond to AI coherently or merely feel compelled to adapt. Consultation depth (${v.consultationDepth}) determines whether institutional learning flows back into governance or remains rhetorical.`; }
mechanismTexts.politics: function(v, summary){ return `Political resistance (${v.politicalResistance}), governance anxiety (${v.governanceAnxiety}) and competitive pressure (${v.targetPressure}) ${summary.kickback.mean > 55 ? 'make rollback, hardening or symbolic consultation likely unless mitigation is strengthened.' : 'are present but currently more containable.'} Mitigation power (${v.mitigationPower}) and consultation depth (${v.consultationDepth}) are the main stabilisers.`; }
